data.title: Bakkt Joins Market Mania But Remains In Cash Burn Reality
data.description:
I've been bearish on Bakkt Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:BKKT) on the back of what's been consistent quarters of net losses, negative free cash flow ("FCF'), and a declining liquidity balance against its crypto operations that have not had a healthy stock market fit. However, in a new market zeitgeist where fundamentals seem to be simply suggestions to be replaced by vibes, BKKT has seen its common equity rally heavily in response to a deluge of positive news. The ticker is set to expand its board with the addition of Mike Alfred, an IREN Limited (IREN) board member and founder of Alpine Fox, a bitcoin-focused private investment fund. IREN owns and operates data centers for AI and mines Bitcoin ("BTC"). The stock rallied heavily on the news, moving to a more than 3-year high, perhaps with the view being that the addition of a new board member will be able to help reverse years of operational decline and compounding losses.

Akshay Naheta, who joined BKKT in March as co-CEO, was also named sole CEO and President. Naheta was previously a senior executive at SoftBank Group (OTCPK:SFTBY), leading on the company's public equity investments and working on some large tentpole deals, including an investment in Nvidia (NVDA) when he joined the Tokyo-based multinational investment company in 2017, before leaving in 2021 to create Distributed Technologies Research. He joined BKKT earlier this year. BKKT reported fiscal 2025 second-quarter revenue of $577.88 million, up 13% over its year-ago comp. Total operating expenses expanded by 12% year-over-year to come in at $596.37 million. Bulls would be right to highlight that this still drove a net loss of $14.7 million that was lower than the year-ago figure, with negative EPS down by 51 cents year-over-year. BKKT's trailing twelve-month FCF position is still negative, but the company is seeing an upswing.

Free Cash Flow, Liquidity, And New Operations

BKKT's second-quarter results are in some ways legacy financials. The company is going through a metamorphosis, divesting its loyalty business for $11 million in a definitive agreement set to close in the third quarter. Critically, the loyalty business, while broadly higher margin than BKKT's core crypto services revenue, was seeing year-over-year declines in revenue. Revenue for the segment during the second quarter dipped by 23% over its year-ago quarter. The money from the sale should help bolster a cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash position, which stood at $61.45 million as of the end of the second quarter.

BKKT also completed the sale of Bakkt Trust to Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) for proceeds of around $4.5 million, generating a loss on the sale of around $2.3 million. The firm in transition also sold 6.75 million of its common shares and warrants post-period end in July to raise $75 million to further develop its BTC as a treasury strategy. The sales were of immaterial operations, and BKKT's highly unprofitable core crypto services business will remain the primary driver of its fundamentals.

Management communicated during the second quarter earnings call that their strategy will hinge on three pillars: (1) Brokerage-in-a-box; (2) Stablecoin payments; and (3) Bitcoin as a treasury. BKKT acquired the domain name ("bitcoin.co.jp") last week in a move management stated will help build the third pillar. While issuing a business wire for a domain name seems unnecessary, BKKT wants to signal to the market that it's a different company. This has underpinned the shift in vibes for a ticker that once had to engineer a 1-for-25 reverse stock split and whose liquidity has been weighed down by back-to-back quarters of negative FCF.

FCF also seems to be on an upswing, which, when aggregated with the recent asset sales, does provide a healthier fundamental backdrop for BKKT. Shareholders seem to be ignoring the dilution for now, with the BKKT also recently redeeming a $25 million outstanding 0.00% convertible debenture due June 18, 2026. BKKT always held little debt, with the recent move helping plump up liquidity. The primary risk continues to coalesce around the direction of FCF and just how deep a cash runway the equity offering, existing cash position, and the proceeds from the sale of the loyalty business will form.

Conclusion

Do I still think BKKT is a sell? This isn't clear. The ongoing rally could have some legs. The CEO will have to prove to the market that the transformation can stem historical cash burn and maintain healthy liquidity levels. Investing in BTC as part of a corporate treasury strategy only works until the market tips back bearish and focus again shifts to underlying fundamentals. I'm rating BKKT as a hold against the clearer shift in operations.

data.title: This Economic Report Spells Goldilocks
data.description:
Stocks took a breather yesterday, as Chairman Powell reminded investors that the Fed remains data dependent from one meeting to the next, and that there is no preset course for monetary policy, despite market expectations for another rate cut at each of the final two meetings of the year. He also noted that the risks to inflation are to the upside, while the risks to the labor market are to the downside, which means that there is “no risk-free path” for policy. His comments weighed on the major market indexes, but the selling looked more like profit taking after a record run rather than a concern about the outlook. To the contrary, I think yesterday’s only economic report spells Goldilocks for the economy.

The S&P Global Flash PMI (purchasing managers index) indicates that business activity slowed for a second consecutive month with the index falling from 54.6 in August to 53.6 in September, but it remains well into expansionary territory. The line of demarcation between expansion and contraction is 50. A softer rate of business activity suggests that the labor market will remain soft, which was corroborated by the sub-index for employment falling to a five-month low. That should keep the Fed in easing mode moving forward, even if it skips a rate cut at its October meeting.

At the same time, while input prices for producers purchasing materials rose to a four-month high, the sub-index for prices received from consumers of end products eased to its lowest level since April. In particular, goods price inflation cooled to the lowest level since January, which is not what I expected at this stage. This suggests that tariffs are still largely being absorbed by businesses and not passed on to consumers en masse. This will clearly take a toll on profit margins, but it will lessen the impact on consumer price inflation, which is the Fed’s primary concern.

A softening labor market combined with a more moderate increase in consumer prices than expected is as good as it gets for the investment landscape. Again, it is a Goldilocks backdrop for the US economy, because it appears that the rate of economic growth will sustain through this period of temporary price increases, allowing the Fed to continue easing monetary policy over the coming 12-month period to a neutral rate of interest. The S&P Global Composite PMI is just one report, but it has been one of the more reliable ones in the post-pandemic period, which is why it is one of my favorite real-time pulses on US economic activity.

data.title: Jazz Pharmaceuticals Announces Promising Xywav Data: Why I Assign A BUY Rating
data.description:
Jazz Pharmaceuticals plc (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:JAZZ) has just announced some significant milestone at a presentation at the 38th annual Psych Congress in San Diego held from Sept. 17 to Sept. 21, 2025. The company has unveiled promising results from its Phase 4 DUET trial for Xywav, emphasizing its efficacy in treating narcolepsy and idiopathic hypersomnia. I believe the DUET trial strengthens Xywav’s clinical profile, proving benefits for both daytime symptoms and nighttime sleep. I see this bolstering Jazz’s market leadership as well as supporting long-term adoption. Additionally, this secures growth as Xyrem’s patents expire, protecting their core neuroscience franchise. Due to the latest announcement, I assign a BUY rating on JAZZ stock.

Jazz Pharmaceuticals is a global biopharmaceutical company dedicated to bringing life-changing medicines to people with limited or no options, so they can live their lives more fully. The company has a diverse portfolio of marketed medicines including leading therapies for sleep disorders and epilepsy, and a growing portfolio of cancer treatments.

How Jazz Operates

Jazz Pharmaceuticals exists to help patients with serious, often rare diseases where treatment options are limited.

The company focusses on neuroscience where it addresses challenges to do with sleep disorders like narcolepsy and idiopathic hypersomnia plus epilepsy. Besides neuroscience, Jazz is also in the oncology space where it deals with hard-to-treat cancers includng small cell lung cancer, rare brain tumours and certain blood cancers.

The company then generates revenue by developing and selling high-value specialty drugs. These drugs are prescribed by physicians and provided to patients through specialty pharmacies, hospitals, and treatment centres.

Meanwhile, the company's current biggest revenue drivers are Xywav, the leading therapy for narcolepsy and idiopathic hypersomnia, and Epidiolex, a cannabis-derived epilepsy treatment expected to achieve blockbuster status. Its cancer medicines also contribute to revenue although growth is more variable.

Jazz serves small patient populations and because of that, drug prices are high, with insurance companies, government payers, and health systems bearing most of the cost. The company's success relies on innovation and new approvals.

Current Commercial Products

Jazz Pharmaceuticals’ current growth has been anchored by two leading neuroscience products namely Xywav and Epidiolex.

Xywav, introduced in 2020 as a low-sodium successor to Xyrem, addresses safety concerns around long-term sodium exposure while extending Jazz’s oxybate franchise. The drug has quickly become the company’s number one product as it generated $415 million in Q2 2025 sales with 15,200 active patients.

Xywav is now the market leader in narcolepsy and the only FDA-approved treatment for idiopathic hypersomnia (IH), a condition with previously no approved therapy. The company's Xyrem sales have been rapidly declining and Xywav is expected is the replacement and expected to keep growing. The drug is supported by increasing IH diagnoses and patent protection into the 2030s.

Meanwhile, Epidiolex is expected to be the next real thing. The drug was developed by GW Pharmaceuticals and acquired by Jazz in 2021 for $7 billion. It is the first FDA-approved cannabis-derived therapy, initially for rare childhood epilepsies. So far, the drug has been approved in over 35 countries and it generated $252 million in Q2.

Epidiolex is set to surpass blockbuster status this year, with future upside from adult use and label expansions.

The Significance of Latest Update

I believe that Jazz's recent DUET trial results, presented at the Psych Congress in San Diego, mark an important milestone for the company.

The presentation confirmed that Xywav, the company's flagship low-sodium oxybate, not only reduces daytime symptoms such as excessive sleepiness but also improves nighttime sleep in adults with narcolepsy and idiopathic hypersomnia.

It was discovered that patients with narcolepsy experienced fewer awakenings, while IH patients reported less sleep inertia, the grogginess felt upon waking. Meanwhile, additional findings suggested benefits at higher doses, further strengthening Xywav’s clinical profile.

I think that these results are strategically important for Jazz as they reinforce the long-term value of Xywav, particularly with patents on its older, high-sodium predecessor Xyrem nearing expiration. In my view, this positive data can enhance Jazz’s competitive positioning, support physician confidence, and expand market share, offsetting ongoing financial pressures.

Jazz's Pipeline Projects

2025 is a catalyst-heavy year for the company with two FDA decisions (dordaviprone, Zepzelca 1L) plus major Ziihera Phase 3 data.

Dordaviprone, a first-in-class small molecule for recurrent H3 K27M-mutant diffuse glioma, a devastating paediatric and young adult brain tumour, received FDA approval in August 2025 and carries patent protection through 2037, representing Jazz’s key rare oncology bet.

On the other hand, Zepzelca which is already marketed for second-line small cell lung cancer (SCLC), is under FDA review for first-line maintenance in extensive-stage SCLC with a decision due October 7, 2025.

Ziihera (zanidatamab), a HER2-targeted antibody, has conditional EU approval for second-line biliary tract cancer and it generated $6 million in Q2 sales. Additionally, top-line results from its Phase 3 HERIZON-GEA-01 trial in first-line gastroesophageal adenocarcinoma are expected in Q4 2025, with Jazz positioning it as a potential “HER2 agent of choice.”

Looking to 2026–27, Jazz is advancing Ziihera in HER2-positive breast cancer through the Phase 2 EmpowHER-BC-208 trial and seeking to expand Rylaze. This one has already been approved in paediatrics, into adolescent and young adult acute lymphoblastic leukemia.

Beyond 2027, Ziihera’s broader development spans pan-tumour, breast, and gastrointestinal cancers, aiming to move from niche rare cancers into mainstream HER2-driven markets. Jazz consistently highlights these programs as 'blockbuster potential' and 'practice-changing,' though without publishing revenue forecasts.

Jazz's Potential Risks

Jazz faces several key risks that could challenge its growth trajectory and investor confidence.

The company's over-reliance on its two neuroscience blockbusters, Xywav and Epidiolex, is something I could wish away for Jazz. While Xywav enjoys patent protection into the 2030s, I believe that payer pushback or eventual generic challenges could erode exclusivity. Also, Epidiolex’s expansion into adult epilepsy faces competition and reimbursement hurdles.

Oncology, being the company’s diversification strategy, I think carries significant execution risk. Zepzelca’s first-line SCLC FDA decision is pivotal, while Ziihera must compete with entrenched HER2 therapies and prove itself in upcoming Phase 3 and confirmatory trials.

On the other hand, dordaviprone, though approved, serves an ultra-rare indication and may have limited commercial scale in my view. Beyond pipeline uncertainty, Jazz must deal with regulatory risks from potential trial delays or negative outcomes.

Financially, the company has its own struggles including declining operating margins, negative net margins, and a heavy debt load of around $5 billion. This debt came from the GW Pharma acquisition and I believe that such high debt levels constrain strategic flexibility.

That said, I also view the company's high drug prices exposing Jazz to payer resistance, particularly in the U.S. and Europe.

Executive Changes

Jazz’s leadership transition was formally announced on July 10, 2025, with Renee Gala set to become President and CEO effective August 11, 2025. I believe the appointment reflected a deliberate and well-planned succession process, ensuring stability at a critical stage in the company’s evolution. Gala brought continuity and proven leadership, having served as CFO since 2020, then President and COO from 2023, where she drove nearly 90% revenue growth, broadened Jazz’s portfolio, and strengthened its R&D pipeline.

While all CEO transitions carry some execution risk, I think her deep operational, financial, and strategic experience positions her strongly to sustain momentum, unlock pipeline value and reinforce investor confidence in Jazz’s long-term growth trajectory.

Jazz Cash Management

As of Q2 2025, the company had $1.7 billion in cash and around $5.4 billion in debt. I believe such a position provided a solid liquidity base to support near-term product launches and pipeline milestones.

With Xywav and Epidiolex generating over $660 million in Q2 sales, recurring cash inflows help offset elevated R&D and SG&A spend. However, I think cash burn remains a concern as negative net margins and declining operating margins show that heavy investment is pressuring profitability.

Based on current resources, Jazz appears to have sufficient runway through at least 2026 to fund dordaviprone commercialization, Zepzelca expansion, and Ziihera trials. That said, if oncology programs require broader Phase 3 expansions or if revenue growth slows, I think that additional funding could be necessary by 2026–27. Execution on near-term launches will be crucial in determining whether Jazz can internally fund growth or must seek external financing.

Valuation

Jazz trades at a forward Price/Sales ratio of 1.85, well below the broader biotech industry average of 3.95 and far below its neuroscience competitor Axsome Therapeutics at 9.30. I think this discount suggests the market is pricing Jazz more cautiously, reflecting concerns about margin pressure, debt burden, and oncology execution risks, despite strong cash-generating assets like Xywav and Epidiolex. In contrast, Axsome’s premium multiple reflects investor optimism in its growth pipeline, though with higher risk. I also believe that Jazz’s low valuation could signal undervaluation if pipeline catalysts and earnings execution improve.

Conclusion

In summary, I maintain a BUY rating on Jazz Pharmaceuticals. The company is anchored by Xywav’s durable growth and Epidiolex’s blockbuster trajectory, both providing steady cash flow to support oncology pipeline expansion. I think that the latest positive DUET trial data strengthens Xywav’s profile, while upcoming catalysts like Zepzelca’s FDA decision and Ziihera’s Phase 3 readout offer meaningful upside. Despite challenges around debt, margins, and execution risk, Jazz’s cash position, diversified portfolio and well-planned leadership transition provide resilience. With shares trading at a notable discount to peers, I see strong risk-reward and long-term value creation potential.

data.title: ASML: Finally, The Market Is Giving It The Respect It Deserves (Downgrade)
data.description:
ASML: Keep The Faith In Good Stocks

I've always had the conviction that as long as we invest in fundamentally strong companies while they are undervalued, the market will subsequently discover the folly of their skeptical ways, which should then lead to a re-rating in these stocks. Such a play arguably transpired in the stock of ASML Holding N.V. (NASDAQ:ASML), as I upgraded the stock in July, communicating my views that the market became too pessimistic back then on its growth recovery prospects.

And guess what, as I pen this update, ASML has risen in quick succession in the past three weeks since bottoming above the $685 level in late July. The buying fervor has also spurred a substantive outperformance over the S&P 500 (SPX) since my last update, as investors loaded up quickly. With the recent rise, ASML has inched much closer to the $1,000 level, reaching levels last seen in July 2024, a noteworthy vote of confidence for dip buyers who bought when the rest looked away.

ASML: Finally Getting The Traction In AI

If you have been following ASML's thesis closely, I'm pretty sure I don't have to repeat the series of AI infrastructure deals that are expected to benefit the whole wafer fab equipment value chain. While ASML's recent decision to invest €1.3 billion into Europe's leading AI company, Mistral AI, is notable, that's not expected to move the needle on the market's prognosis on ASML's bullish narrative, as compared to what has ensued in the U.S. market over the past couple of weeks.

After all, while ASML's investment values Mistral at €10 billion in pre-money valuation, it pales in comparison to what OpenAI or Anthropic are currently worth based on the most recent funding rounds. Therefore, while I view ASML's ability to leverage its robust profitability and sound financial footing to further integrate with the AI value chain, I don't expect material changes to its financial outlook in the near- or medium-term.

However, things look very different in Silicon Valley, as Nvidia (NVDA) inked a $5 billion deal to invest in Intel (INTC), potentially tapping into a reportedly $50 billion TAM. Nvidia is rapidly spreading its wings into the data center and PC business, working in unison with Intel on the x86 architecture. It has clearly reignited the market sentiments on INTC, and it appears that ASML's buying momentum has also been upgraded markedly.

ASML has been under substantive pressure over the past year given the challenges facing TSMC's (TSM) foundry competitors in the name of Intel and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF). Intel's malaise follows a series of poor execution, damaging its credibility in keeping pace with TSMC, while Samsung has faced questions on its HBM roadmap, ceding market leadership to SK Hynix and Micron (MU) in the HBM3E space. These are ASML's most significant customers. Thus, for the market to accord a valuation premium predicated on ASML's EUV dominance, the market needs substantially more robust performances showing solid traction.

Therefore, ASML investors must have breathed a sigh of relief, as the worst in the technological progress and business performance for Samsung and Intel appear to be behind us. Samsung has reportedly cleared Nvidia's validation phase for HBM3E, propelling its recent buying fervor, which also saw the stock approach its July 2024 highs. In addition, Nvidia's investment in Intel has also raised optimism that it might even consider leveraging Intel's foundry for its future chips, although it's far too early for us to ascertain any concrete plans.

ASML: EUV Outlook Is Key To A Further Upgrade

For ASML, I believe it has revived hopes in the market that could see more aggressive investments for EUV tools in the longer term, as the AI growth prospects become more prevalent across its leading foundry customers. And it might also help lift optimism beyond the hyperscaler-led growth. Apple's (AAPL) grand return with the iPhone 17 series has also capped a phenomenal recovery for the Cupertino company, as investors then worried about whether Tim Cook and his team have a viable roadmap with on-device AI.

With EUV systems revenue accounting for almost 45% of ASML's total net system sales revenue over the past 12 months, it could rejuvenate its AI thesis that seems to have eluded ASML investors so far (as compared to the other semiconductor bellwethers like TSMC, Nvidia, and Broadcom (AVGO)).

Also, I think it should help to mitigate the geopolitical concerns over the normalization in revenue upside for ASML, as China's chipmakers pulled forward their spending recently. While the trade disagreements between the United States and China look well-primed to thaw further following recent talks between President Trump and President Xi Jinping, potential flare-ups on the semiconductor supply chain could still stymie another round of aggressive purchases by the Chinese companies.

Nevertheless, the shroud over ASML's underperforming leading-edge customers (Intel and Samsung) has conceivably been lifted, affording more conviction in their spending trajectory. While I don't expect ASML to telegraph a much quicker growth inflection in the short run, I believe it has lent credence to the clarity of its outlook, lowering the risks of further downward adjustments moving ahead.

However, there are still valid concerns about whether the surge has driven ASML into overvalued zones right now. Based on its forward EBITDA multiple of 27x, it's markedly above the 10-year average of 24x, so I think it's justified to say that ASML has moved well past its then undervalued zones.

Yet, the valuation remains well below the highs notched in 2021 and early 2024, when its forward EBITDA surged over the 35x level. Those proved unsustainable as they were subsequently digested.

Buy Or Sell ASML Stock?

A bearish thesis is not reasonable, as the market has yet to fully reflect the optimism, in my opinion. The ongoing geopolitical cross-currents should continue to impede such an accelerated valuation upgrade.

However, after a dramatic spike, as seen in ASML's price action above, we must also be more cautious when trying to assess the potential for a near-term overshoot. Hence, a more appropriate posture is to perhaps allow management more time to provide their views about the recent developments at its next earnings call before thinking about whether to buy the next pullback.

Investors who managed to add aggressively at ASML's previous dips should consider riding the recovery, notwithstanding the possibility of a reversal, given its overbought surge.

Rating: Downgrade to "Hold."

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Centrus Energy: Strike When Ready For Decades Of Growth To Come
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Centrus Energy (NYSE:LEU), the stock has gained nearly 530% in price. I was wrong about this stock, and I'll tell you why.

A friend of my Dad raised Centrus Energy and a few other nuclear energy stocks to me a year ago. I said I'd take a look, and I published an analysis on the article called "Centrus Energy: Investors Already Realize Nuclear's Significance." I get way more calls right than wrong, maybe it's 80/20—80% right and 20% wrong—but in this instance, I was firmly in the 20%; I was wrong.

The important thing is to recognize it, and figure out why. Thankfully, my good picks still have me up by 42% year-to-date versus 18% for the Nasdaq-100, and that's while I've held a 20% cash position. None of us are perfect, the game of greatness is in recognizing our errors, and then course correcting to get what we really deserve.

Based on my updated analysis of Centrus, these are the entry points that were appealing versus unappealing:

My rating back in July 2024 should have been an unequivocal Strong Buy. I should have bought it. So why didn't I? Value. The principle that can keep you average if you don't understand sentiment.

In other words, what you deem as fair value is not what the market does, and the market doesn't care about your opinion. So how do you beat the market? You predict what the market will think next, or my favorite way of saying it: "the best way to beat the market is to successfully predict the flow of funds."

Fundamentals

Centrus is a thematic investment, but it's also grounded in real fundamentals, including strong supply constrains and policy anchoring. Centrus produced the first U.S. HALEU (High-Assay Low-Enriched Uranium) and reached the 900 kg milestone under its DOE contract. The stock is not just future "hype" based on long-term potential; Centrus is now in the first year of the DOE option period running to June 30, 2026, and it has options potentially beyond this.

As of June 30, 2025, Centrus disclosed $3.6B total backlog, which includes $2.7B LEU and about $0.9B in technical solutions. In addition, DOE communications and analyses put HALEU needs anywhere from ~50 metric tons/year by 2035 to ~600 metric tons/year by 2035, depending on how aggressive the build-out becomes. In any case, the ceiling is high and supported by strong policy.

Perhaps the strongest form of policy supporting Centrus stock right now, which has been a factor for a while (including during the time of my prior analysis), is that the Prohibiting Russian Uranium Imports Act bans Russian LEU with limited waivers and unlocks about $2.72 billion that Congress has appropriated to re-build U.S. fuel supply. The IRA also has a $700M HALEU Availability Program, and these two aspects are the foundation of the policy tailwinds supporting the investment theme.

Centrus's balance sheet has $833 milion in cash with total current liabilites of $482.2 million. This means debt service is not crushing to the investment case and liquidity is strong. The company also redeemed old 8.25% notes recently, so it is de-leveraging, but there is a proposed $650 million 2032 convertibles outlined, which raises future coupon and dilution risk.

Energy Competition

This is the investment case for Centrus stock laid out in simple first principles. Firstly, there's only one U.S. HALEU producer: Centrus. Secondly, Washington is already banning imports and funding domestic enrichment. Thirdly, rising electricity loads and decarbonization mean that nuclear is shifting from an optional energy form to a necessary one. Finally, the upside case is also asymmetric, because its backlog covers near-term stability and if advanced reactors take off, Centrus is the U.S. first-mover supplier in the highest medium-term potential niche of nuclear: HALEU.

Due to the importance of the thematic quality of this investment, we should also focus on competition versus other energy forms from a macro perspective.

Renewables like solar and wind have the lowest marginal cost, rapid build-outs, and are politically popular globally. However, the energy is intermittent, storage costs are high, and there are grid integration issues. Realistically, nuclear energy is not going to replace baseload, but nuclear will cleverly support renewables by providing constant energy stability.

Natural gas is cheap, flexible, and entrenched in the U.S. power mix, but there are heavy carbon emissions and geopolitical price swings. I think natural gas will play a strong competitive role in the short run, but I believe in a clean climate, too. Resultantly, climate policy will inevitably cap the role of nat gas. That's a long-term upside lever for nuclear's significance.

Nuclear fusion is a very interesting long-term competitor. The process joins light atomic nuclei (like hydrogen isotopes) to release energy, which is the same reaction that powers the sun. At scale, this could basically deliver limitless carbon-free baseload power, and it isn't weather-dependent. However, given the complexity of scaling such operations effectively, we're likely decades away from this. Centrus and its enriched uranium for fission reactors (splitting heavy atomic nuclei to release energy) is currently the most stable investment case for the next 10–15 years. One important lesson in investing, you don't want to be so ahead of the curve that you're "wrong in the moment." Being early will get you ahead; being too early won't.

Valuation

Centrus has a forward price-to-sales ratio of 12, which is 790% higher than the sector median. That might look daunting on the surface, but its forward revenue growth is also 815% higher than the sector median. So initially, from the outset, that makes me believe (and rightly so) that this investment is fairly valued.

The tricky point investors have to acclimate to is that Centrus does not have stable earnings growth in the near term. It may take 5–10 years before we see the high bottom-line growth rates that validate this top-line-based valuation. That is why, primarily, even though structural growth is there, this remains a future-oriented thematic investment rather than a stable cash flow business to buy a stake in without reservation.

Remember that a big part of Centrus's growth story depends on government orders, domestic policy, regulatory approvals, and funding (such as from the DOE). There's a big risk of capex drag and of delays, and general lumpiness in demand until nuclear reaches mainstream adoption (which it hasn't yet but I believe in the next 10 years will do).

In essence, even though this is a long-term growth-story hybrid stock, which shouldn't be a core holding, it can certainly be held at <3% with confidence that you're buying an effective long-term leader in LEU production in the U.S. Volatility is to be expected, but high beta means high alpha if you pick right (if you're thematically well-aligned, you usually get back in returns whatever volatility you can tolerate and endure, which is a central tenet to how I operate my Invictus Hydra portfolio).

That said, right now, the 14-day RSI is 76, so it's just common sense to wait for a pullback. In the range of 50–60 I'll probably initiate a tester position. At an RSI of sub-50 I may double that. At an RSI of below 30 I'll fill a 3–4% weight position. Annual returns from this valuation will not be as astronomical as the last 12 months, but realistically we can see stable 20–30% CAGRs if upward revisions continue.

Core Risks

There are timing risks to LEU's revenues which could introduce substantial volatility to the return horizon. The DOE's HALEU contract is cost-plus-incentive with milestone billing, which means that Centrus spends first and is reimbursed later. Also, option years are at DOE's discretion and are funded only if Congress appropriates money, so budget gridlock could push payments out by quarters. This is why Centrus stock needs to be considered as a high-volatility long-term investment, or traded amid volatility cycles.

Additionally, Centrus has shown it can make HALEU in small amounts (900kg), but it still hasn't proven the much larger volumes that the market will eventually need. At the moment, all of the production rests on one plant in Ohio. So if there's a technical glitch, a safety issue or a regulatory problem, everything stops and there's no backup facility. Inevitably in such a scenario Centrus would probably lose its edge as the only trusted U.S. supplier.

Then we have the risk of Centrus' valuation multiples contracting as other players enter the market. Urenco USA has NRC approval to enrich 10% LEU+ and is already on DOE's contracting framework. Expanding to 19.75% HALEU is probable in due course. Moreover, Orano announced plans for a U.S. enrichment plant in Oak Ridge, TN, trageting the early 2030s. Centrus' valuation is definitely currently supported by it being the only LEU source, but that's about to change, so growth will need to offset any total-dominance narrative here (and I think such growth will sustain sentiment just fine even if some volatility is exhibited due to competition factors).

Lastly, revenues from HALEU could remain small for several years, and this is evidenced by the fact that advanced reactor developers like TerraPower, X-energy, and Oklo (OKLO) are delayed. Specifically and as an example, TerraPower's Natrium slipped from 2028 to around 2030 citing HALEU supply chain constraints. So Centrus may be more confined to conventional LEU sales and DOE stopgap funding more than the market currently anticipates (and is implied in Centrus' valuation).

One last note: short interest is currently ~21%, which is huge. Don't buy now, but don't be afraid to buy at the next significant drawdown. Just be strategic in your overall portfolio allocation, which is the main and overarching pre-requisite to beating the market sustainably.

Conclusion: Hold

I do like this investment, I can't deny. Perhaps I'm a little bit gutted I missed the earlier opportunity, but beyond psychological impulse, the fundamentals and macro narrative really do still align for long-term alpha. My main concern is the valuation impeding returns if competition increases and there are delays or pauses in revenue ramping. That said, this isn't exactly a stock for people who want to sleep well at night—it's for hardcore long-term volatility investors seeking aggressive alpha. That might be you, and if it is, still have the patience to buy this stock when the RSI is more favorable. At this very moment buying Centrus stock is just silly, but in a few months the price may be not only fine, but strategically elite for entry.

data.title: CES Energy Solutions: Still Attractive Despite Strong YTD Share Price Performance
data.description:
Introduction

CES Energy Solutions Corp. (TSX:CEU:CA, OTCPK:CESDF) is a Canadian company focusing on the development and production of consumable fluids and specialty chemicals for the North American oil and gas industry. The company is active throughout the USA and Canada (with 71% of the Q2 revenue generated in the USA), and has exposure to several segments of the oil and gas industry. The image below shows where CES is active, and which trade names it has in its portfolio.

The company serves a wide range of clients, with approximately 55% of its total revenue coming from customers with a market cap of in excess of $10B, ranging up to market caps of $700B.

The company currently has 219M shares outstanding, resulting in a market capitalization of C$1.9B, based on the current share price of approximately C$8.8.

A strong earnings and cash flow profile

The company’s financial results are subject to seasonal changes as the second quarter of the year represents the spring break-up period, resulting in the lowest level of activities throughout the year. The winter drill period tends to be the most active, while the summer months have ‘medium’ activity levels. This means we shouldn’t extrapolate quarterly results to derive the full-year expectations and although I’ll discuss the recently published Q2 results, I will subsequently take a step back and look at the H1 result in general.

In the second quarter of the year, CES reported a total revenue of almost C$574M, which allowed it to report an operating profit of just under C$59M. As the company also recorded a net finance income, its pre-tax profit was boosted to C$64M, on which just over C$12M in taxes was owed. This resulted in a net profit of C$51.8M for an EPS of C$0.23.

As you can see in the image above, the net finance income was a nice tailwind, as the company had reported a net finance expense of approximately C$17.5M in the first quarter of the year. As you can see below, that (likely non-recurring) finance income item was related to a C$17.4M FX gain, partially offset by a C$3.4M derivative loss.

As the image above also shows, the "pure" interest expenses are approximately C$8.5M per quarter and thus around C$17M per semester. This indeed means the recorded net finance cost of C$12.4M in the entire first semester is a better (but still not perfect) representation of the underlying situation.

That’s also why I like to keep an eye on a company’s cash flow statement as well. As you can see below, CES reported an operating cash flow of C$76.7M before taking changes in the working capital position into account. But from that number we still have to deduct C$11.3M in lease payments. This means the underlying operating cash flow was C$65.4M.

The total capex incurred during the second quarter was approximately C$25.2M (excluding the C$7M cash outflow related to an acquisition), indicating a net free cash flow of C$40M. Divided over the 219M shares outstanding (and this number is decreasing), CES Energy Solutions reported a FCFPS of C$0.23 in the second quarter of the year, and approximately C$0.34 per share in the entire first half of the year.

That sounds a bit light, but keep in mind the timing of the capital expenditures plays a role here. CES spent in excess of C$58M on capex items and almost C$21M on lease payments for a total of C$79M versus the total depreciation and amortization expenses of just over C$50M.

It is important to realize CES has been guiding for a full-year capex of C$80M (which means almost 75% has already been spent in the first semester), with only half of that (C$40M) representing maintenance capex. This means the normalized maintenance capex incurred in the first half of the year would be C$20M on a pro forma basis.

And that’s important to put the weak FCFPS into perspective. On average, only C$20M of the C$58M in incurred capex would be deemed maintenance capex. And using that number, the underlying sustaining free cash flow result would be approximately C$113M or C$0.51 per share. On an annual basis, we are looking at a FCF yield of approximately 13-14%.

The balance sheet remains robust - allowing for share repurchases

The very strong underlying cash flow result allows CES to invest in growth (given the anticipated C$40M in growth capex this year) while aggressively buying back its own shares.

That’s enabled by the robust balance sheet. At the end of the second quarter, CES Energy Solutions had a positive working capital position of in excess of C$600M while its net debt (excluding lease liabilities) was just C$374M. C$200M of this amount comes from the 2029 senior notes while C$168M was drawn from the senior credit facility (with an additional C$382M available to draw down). And as you can see below, the company comfortably meets all covenants.

CES Energy Solutions currently pays a quarterly dividend of just C$0.0425 per share for C$0.17 per share per year, representing a dividend yield of less than 2%. A large portion of its underlying free cash flow is spent on share buybacks, having spent C$31.3M in Q2 and C$52.6M in the entire first half of the year. This allowed the company to reduce its net share count by approximately 5.4M shares, and CES may very well end the year with a net share count of just around 215M shares. It goes without saying this will further enhance the per-share metrics.

Investment thesis

CES Energy Solutions has an enviable position in the drilling fluids market, and is currently participating in some larger tender offers (and it expects to win a few of those). About 75% of the tender offers it is currently participating in are from potential new clients which will allow CES to gain some market share. The company has been hiring additional employees that will allow them to hit the ground running upon winning a/multiple tenders which also means that if CES would not win any new contracts, the G&A expenses will likely decrease again as the headcount will be right-sized again.

I currently have no position in CES Energy Solutions, but I am definitely keeping an eye on the stock now. The oil services segment is cheap in general, but I like having exposure to the chemicals too, rather than just owning drilling & completion services.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Valuation Vs. Reality: Why Firefly Aerospace's Stock Faces Significant Downside
data.description:
Space and defense technology company Firefly Aerospace Inc. (NASDAQ:FLY) stands at a critical juncture. While it possesses undeniable strengths that have positioned it as a significant player in the rapidly growing global space economy, a rigorous financial analysis reveals severe underlying challenges. Despite its rapid revenue growth, Firefly is plagued by deep and worsening losses, a very high customer concentration, and a cash burn rate that threatens its financial stability. The company’s valuation implies growth expectations that are historically improbable and fundamentally unsustainable. Consequently, I assign a “Sell” rating to the company, as the great optimism embedded in its valuation fails to account for the significant execution risks and competitive pressures it faces.

Firefly’s Strategic Challenges in the Global Space Economy

Firefly provides responsive and reliable mission solutions. Its key offerings are the Alpha launch vehicle, which has a proven flight record, and the larger, in-development Eclipse rocket. Its success, including a moon landing, makes it a “partner of choice for national security, government, and commercial customers”.

The growing space economy, which encompasses “launch, spacecraft, satellite constellations, and on-orbit and in-space services”, has become increasingly attractive, with competition breaking down along the following lines: providers of dedicated and rideshare launch vehicles; providers of spacecraft and satellite constellations, and providers of launch and spacecraft solutions for on-orbit and in-space services. Management cites a 2024 McKinsey & Company report which says that,

We estimate that the global space economy will be worth $1.8 trillion by 2035 (accounting for inflation), up from $630 billion in 2023. This figure includes both “backbone” applications—such as those for satellites, launchers, and services like broadcast television or GPS—and what we term “reach” applications”—those for which space technology helps companies across industries generate revenues. Uber, for example, relies on the combination of satellite signals and chips inside smartphones to connect drivers and riders and provide directions in every city.

In 2023, backbone applications made up $330 billion, or slightly greater than 50 percent, of the global space economy, while reach applications represented $300 billion. The expected annual growth rate for backbone and reach applications is twice the projected rate of GDP growth over the next decade (exhibit). For comparison, the value estimates for space are similar to those for semiconductors (estimated at $600 billion in 2021 with 6 to 8 percent annual growth into the 2030s) and roughly half of the projection for the global payments industry (estimated to reach $3.2 trillion in revenues by 2027).

Firefly’s growth in the global space economy seems to be a consequence of its being one of the few US commercial firms who can reliably provide access to launch, transit and operations in space. Even without the reshoring that has begun, given the national security importance of the global space economy, the company is likely to see some growth.

However, it is almost a law of capital that the prospect of attractive returns draws in new capital, and this is true of the global space economy. In fulfilling its mission, the company faces enormous competition in the global space industry, competition that has only grown with time. As the company explained in its S-1/A,

Competitors across our launch, land, and orbit businesses range in size from divisions of large public corporations to small, privately held entities. We face intense competition in the global space market. In addition, we are aware of a significant number of competitors actively engaged in developing commercial launch capabilities for small and medium sized payloads in the U.S. and globally. Many of our current and potential competitors are larger and have substantially greater financial or other resources than we currently have or expect to have in the future, and thus may be better positioned to exploit the market need for spacecraft propulsion systems and launch services for small and medium sized payloads with targeted orbital delivery, which is one primary focus of our launch business.

Even foreign competition could hurt the company domestically, by constraining the price that the company could set for its services. At present, the company has significant competitive advantages with its unique "one-stop shop" model offering launch vehicles, landers, and orbital spacecraft; its patented technology, a record of rapid-response missions for national security; and a robust $1.1 billion backlog backed by strong partnerships with major agencies and defense contractors. In addition, the company’s vertically integrated, purpose-built infrastructure is a formidable barrier to entry. Nevertheless, as the industry consolidates, and new capital enters these competitive advantages are under threat. With increased competition will come an eroding of NOPAT margins, and a decline in return on invested capital (ROIC).

Customer Concentration is High

In its S-1/A, management noted that for “the three months ended March 31, 2025, our top five customers together accounted for over 99% of our revenue and our top five backlog customers accounted for approximately 92% of our backlog as of March 31, 2025.” The risks are obvious: any diminishment in volumes from these customers will have severe consequences for the firm’s overall revenues. In addition, because the firm is so dependent on so few customers, it is in a weak negotiating position when it comes to pricing negotiations, making it improbable that the firm can achieve the kind of net operating profit after tax (NOPAT) margin expansion that it requires to justify the current price.

In addition, in an era in which the federal government is scaling back its commitments, it is notable that,

A substantial amount of our backlog for government customers is also subject to risks of future government funding levels, which may be substantially curtailed or abandoned, resulting in contract cancellations, modifications, delays, or reduction in orders. In particular, the current administration has indicated it is committed to decreasing federal spending and the size of government. If the administration were to take actions that impacted the amount our government customers are able to spend on our services, it could materially adversely impact our business, results of operations, and financial condition.

Lacking the kind of customer diversification needed for a robust revenue stream, Firefly’s business is potentially fragile. Indeed, with both Republicans and Democrats seeking to reduce spending, management emphasised repeatedly in its prospectus that future federal government funding cuts "could directly affect our financial performance and could have a material adverse effect on our business, results of operations, prospects, and financial condition.”

Rapid Revenue Growth Amid Persistent Profitability Challenges

Academic research finds that IPO firms, pre-IPO, typically enjoy high levels of growth, and that post-IPO, their growth tends to decline. While one cannot say if decline will be true of Firefly, what one can say is that, since 2023, it has compounded its revenue by a remarkable 36.42% a year, from $55.24 million in 2023 to $102.81 million in the last twelve months (LTM). In the company’s Q2 2025 earnings release, management guided for full-year 2025 revenue of between $133 and $145 million, indicating that revenue at least will continue to grow for the near-term.

The firm’s NOPAT, however, has steadily declined, from -$131.88 million to -$221.69 million in that time. The company’s struggles with profitability are also fairly atypical: academic research shows that 85% of US-based IPOs in 2024 were unprofitable. While NOPAT has declined, the firm’s NOPAT margin improved from -238.75% in 2023 to -215.64% in the LTM. The company’s balance sheet efficiency similarly declined in that time, with invested capital turns dipping from 0.13 to 0.11.

As a growing, unprofitable company investing heavily in expanding the business, the company’s free cash flows (FCF) are obviously negative, declining from -$501.11 million in 2023 to -$386.66 million in the LTM. The company’s LTM FCF has an unattractive yield of -5.3%. The company’s cash burn is significant, with Firefly burning the equivalent of 18.84% of its current market cap, since 2023. (In terms of GAAP FCF that cash burn is much lower, at $545.18 million, or 8.99% of current market cap).

Since Alfred Marshall first published the Principles of Economics in 1890, economists have known that a firm creates value when it earns a ROIC in excess of its cost of capital. Firefly has not been able to pass this “one dollar test”. In other words, a dollar invested in the company would have been better off in the market. While ROIC has risen from -30.89% in 2023 to -22.88% in the LTM, in the LTM, the company has earned -$305.90 million in economic profits.

Market Price Implies Extreme Growth Expectations

At the current price, Firefly has an economic book value (EBV) of -$3.5 billion. EBV is the company’s value today if it didn’t create any value from new investments. In other words, Firefly's entire market capitalization is based on expectations for future value creation. It is important to remember that a company’s value is made up of two things: what it is worth before management undertakes any value-creating activities, its EBV, and the present value of growth opportunities (PVGO), the extra value the market expects from future growth.

Knowing that a share price carries with it assumptions about the performance of operating value drivers, revenue growth, NOPAT margins, and the incremental investment rate, I used my reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) model to estimate these price-implied expectations and stress-test them. In all scenarios, I assume that the Firefly maintains its current incremental investment rate. The LTM incremental working capital investment rate is fairly high at 151.36%, and the reader might wonder if it will not fall. I freeze the rate in order to focus on revenue and NOPAT margin. While there is no quantitative guidance on future investment rates, management remarks in the prospectus that,

The growth of our business is dependent on our ability to improve our profit margins over time while successfully scaling our business, including through continued investment in initiatives to improve our operating leverage.

Later, it adds that,

To continue gaining market share and attracting customers, we plan to continue to make substantial investments in R&D for the continued enhancements of our Launch and Spacecraft Solutions. Our growth opportunity is dependent on our continued ability to expand our addressable launch market, win lunar and orbital missions and expand our portfolio of services related to those offerings.

Without any signals of a material change in direction in terms of investment, the simplest modelling decision is to hold the investment rate constant. Indeed, as investment rates are often driven by wider industry effects, revenue and NOPAT margin more clearly reflect managerial decisions. A much lower investment rate would of course ease the company’s path toward positive FCF and help the company in achieving sustainable profitability. Nevertheless, the assumption of a persistently high investment rate reflects management’s own guidance and aligns with the logic of frontier-market business models, where growth requires heavy reinvestment. The reader may view the accompanying spreadsheet to understand the following logic.

Firstly, if

  • revenue rises to $145 million in 2025, the upper bound of management’s guidance, and compounds by 62.02% a year, thereafter, the firm’s 2-year CAGR at that point, and,

  • NOPAT margin surges immediately to 15%, then,

Firefly has a market-implied competitive advantage period (MICAP) of 12 years, i.e. the market expects Firefly to create value until 2037, at which point, its shareholder value per share equals its current stock price, and the firm posts $47.45 billion in revenue and earns $7.12 billion in NOPAT. In the accompanying spreadsheet, the MICAP year, 2037 is highlighted.

(As the spreadsheet is live, the MICAP is constantly being calculated, so the reader should check at the bottom of the sheet "Price-Implied Expectations ((PIE))", for what the current MICAP is. I have left the periods after that open so that if the MICAP changes as a consequence of a meaningful share price increase, the reader can see what the new MICAP is using the current inputs. This is done only so that the spreadsheet has a long shelf life. It is not meant to indicate that revenue growth and NOPAT margins continue as given. Strictly, the reader should think of the post 2037 period as one in which the company's ROIC equals or is below the cost of capital, i.e. when the firm no longer creates value.)

If, however, these lofty expectations are not met, and,

  • Revenue rises to $133 million in 2025, the lower bound of management’s guidance, and compounds by 55.17% a year thereafter, the firm’s 2-year CAGR at that point, and,

  • NOPAT margin is 15%, then,

Firefly posts $25.9 billion in revenue and earns $3.89 billion in NOPAT in 2037, and the share is worth $17.96 today, a downside of 63.73% from the current price.

Finally, if,

  • Revenue rises to $133 million in 2025, and compounds by 40% a year thereafter, and,

  • NOPAT margin is 15%, then,

Firefly posts $7.54 billion in revenue and earns $1.13 billion in NOPAT in 2037, and the share is worth $1.98 today, a downside of 96% from the current price.

It should be noted that it is highly unlikely that any firm can sustain 40%-plus revenue growth for a decade. According to Credit Suisse’s “The Base Rate Book”, between 1950 and 2015, just 0.5% of firms enjoyed a 10-year sales CAGR of more than 40%. Moreover, in the firm’s prospectus, management warned that,

We expect to continue to incur net losses for the next several years and we may not achieve or maintain profitability in the future.

…We cannot assure you that we will ever achieve or sustain profitability and may continue to incur significant losses going forward. Any failure by us to achieve or sustain profitability on a consistent basis could cause the value of our common stock to decline.

Sell

Although Firefly operates in an attractive market and has shown impressive technical capabilities, capabilities that have spurred its growth, its present valuation is not justifiable. Firefly’s potential fragilities due to heavy customer concentration, increasing competition, and an inability to achieve profitability. Looming large over the company’s prospects is the potential for federal government budget cuts that could impede the company’s growth. As a consequence, there is a large gap between what the company’s operating history suggests it can achieve and what the market expects of it. This results in severe downsides. As a result, I assign a “Sell” rating to the firm.

data.title: Amgen: Imdelltra Could Be The Next Game Changer
data.description:
It's been just over three months since my last article on Amgen Inc. (NASDAQ:AMGN), "Amgen: Imdelltra Data and Q1 Results Impressive."

In it, I mainly presented an analysis of clinical data on its "nascent blockbuster" called Imdelltra, which I have high hopes for as a medication that has the potential to bring Amgen to one of the leading positions in the small-cell lung cancer therapeutics market, which is expected to grow to $23 billion by 2035.

From a technical analysis perspective, Mr. Market continues to hold Amgen's share price near the VWAP [$286.16], preventing either the "bulls" or the "bears" from gaining an advantage and finally breaking out of the tedious corrective pattern called the "triangle" that runs between $255 and $324.

In my opinion, this fluctuation also reflects institutional investors' reluctance to invest in Amgen or the ETFs it's a part of, including VanEck Biotech ETF [Amgen weight is 14.17%] (BBH), iShares Biotechnology ETF [6.54%] (IBB), and Invesco Nasdaq Biotechnology ETF [6.52%] (IBBQ).

But despite this, there are two more important questions that I ask myself as well.

If Amgen achieves success in R&D? If its portfolio of medications expands?

The short answer is yes.

Repatha is expanding its presence in the cardiovascular drugs market

So, one of the latest key milestones that, in my view, will strengthen Amgen's balance sheet in the short term is that on August 25, the FDA approved Repatha for use by people who have high levels of LDL-C, or as it is called "bad cholesterol," and who also have an raised risk for developing MACE, but who have not yet been diagnosed by a cardiologist with a particular cardiovascular disease.

In general, when I looked at the data provided by the CDC, I was deeply saddened by the current situation, as approximately 25 million adults had total cholesterol levels [calculated as LDL-C+HDL-C+20% of triglycerides] above 240 mg/dL, a level that increases the risk of plaque formation inside blood vessels, which can lead to angina, heart attack, or stroke.

As a result, these data only strengthen my belief that Repatha's commercial prospects are bright, despite intensified competition in the PCSK9 inhibitors market from Novartis' Leqvio (NVS) and Sanofi/Regeneron's Praluent (SNY) (REGN).

Despite competition, demand for it remains high.

To be more precise, sales of Amgen's anti-PCSK9 monoclonal antibody reached $696 million in the three months ended June 30, 2025, up 30.8% year-on-year.

Now it's time to discuss Amgen's financial results for Q2 2025, which, among other things, reinforce my confidence that its stock has a more than attractive risk/reward ratio.

Amgen's revenue has hit its all-time high, thanks in part to Tezspire and Evenity

Its revenue reached $9.18 billion in Q2 2025, beating the consensus estimate by about $261.9 million, as well as my best-case scenario by $80 million.

Overall, it increased by 9.4% year-on-year and 12.6% quarter-on-quarter, which, in my opinion, is a clear signal to Wall Street that declining sales of Xgeva, as well as the weak performance of Enbrel [etanercept], caused in part by its lower efficacy relative to AbbVie's Rinvoq/Skyrizi (ABBV) and Eli Lilly's Taltz (LLY) in treating psoriatic arthritis, are no longer having a noticeable impact on Amgen's total revenue.

On the other hand, to the delight of the "bulls," Amgen's non-GAAP earnings demonstrated stronger growth.

So it was $6.02, which is 22.1% more than in the second quarter of 2024, despite the continued increase in Amgen's R&D expenses [$1.45 billion in Q2 2024 vs. $1.74 billion in Q2 2025].

Why are its R&D costs growing?

In my opinion, this is primarily due to the extremely expensive late-stage clinical programs for Imdelltra, rocatinlimab, and its potential competition to Eli Lilly's Mounjaro/Zepbound and Novo Nordisk's Ozempic/Wegovy (NVO) called MariTide, which I discussed in great detail in the article "Amgen Stock: Why I Still Rate It A 'Buy'".

What caused Amgen's revenue and earnings to grow so sharply?

In the table below, I have noted the sales of not only its key medications, but also most of them, to understand and answer this question more comprehensively.

First, in Amgen's osteoporosis franchise, besides Xgeva, which I mentioned earlier, another medication that is beginning to feel pressure from its biosimilars, such as Samsung Bioepis' Ospomyv/Xbryk, Sandoz Group's Wyost/Jubbonti (OTCQX:SDZNY), and Celltrion's Stoboclo/Osenvelt, is Prolia [denosumab].

However, like any Big Pharma, Amgen was no exception in effectively planning its long-term initiatives to offset the damage from the loss of exclusivity of its key drugs [in this article, these are Prolia and Xgeva].

So, the successor to the denosumab franchise was Evenity [romosozumab], approved by the FDA and EMA for the treatment of osteoporosis in postmenopausal women.

Sales of this anti-sclerostin monoclonal antibody, developed jointly with UCB SA (OTCPK:UCBJF), reached $518 million in Q2 2025, an increase of 32.5% year-on-year and 17.2% quarter-on-quarter, and beating my expectations by $25 million.

Now I'll move on to discussing the oncology franchise that Amgen has high hopes for.

In its portfolio, Blincyto and Imdelltra continue to surprise me with strong performance.

So, sales of its "superstar" called Imdelltra [tarlatamab] reached $134 million in the three months ended June 30, 2025, up more than 10-fold year-on-year and 65.4% quarter-on-quarter, in my opinion, mainly due to the results of a Phase 2 DeLLphi-301 study.

To be more precise, it is because the group of patients taking this CD3/DLL3 bispecific antibody had an ORR [objective response rate] of 40%, a median overall survival of 14.3 months, and a more important endpoint, namely, a median duration of response of 9.7 months [confidence interval: 2.7, 20.7+], that it turned out to be more effective in the treatment of extensive-stage small cell lung cancer than Hycamtin and Jazz Pharmaceuticals' Zepzelca (JAZZ).

In addition, I want to note that Opdivo failed in the CheckMate 331 [NCT02481830] and CheckMate 451 [NCT02538666] clinical trials, which led to Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) withdrawing it for this indication on December 29, 2020.

The same thing happened with Keytruda, which failed to demonstrate efficacy in the Phase 3 KEYNOTE-604 trial, which also led to the withdrawal of its indication in the treatment of people with small cell lung cancer a little later, namely on March 1, 2021.

Let's continue.

Since I have already discussed the results of the Phase 3 DeLLphi-304 study in a previous article, I just want to remind you that Imdelltra significantly increased median overall survival by 5.3 months relative to chemotherapy, thereby strengthening my confidence that it is becoming the "gold standard" in the treatment of this type of lung cancer.

Amgen is currently conducting an extensive clinical development program on Imdelltra, evaluating its efficacy as monotherapy and in combination with AstraZeneca's Imfinzi [durvalumab] (AZN), Asher Bio's etakafusp alfa, and MediLink's YL201 for the treatment of various stages of small cell lung cancer, as well as neuroendocrine prostate cancer.

Risks

It's time to discuss the risks associated with Amgen, which range from weaker sales of Kyprolis [a drug used to treat R/R multiple myeloma] than I previously factored into my DCF model to President Trump's continued promises to lower drug prices.

Takeaway

Amgen isn't exactly Mr. Market's favorite yet.

In my opinion, this is partly logical and fairly understandable, as the AI hype isn't fading, while pressure on the pharmaceutical industry remains due to HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.'s actions, which are controversial, as well as the lack of information about what specific steps Trump and the White House will take to reduce drug prices.

However, this political noise is just that, noise, and will only have a short-term impact on Amgen.

Overall, in addition to the investment theses I discussed earlier in the article, another one is that Amgen raised its 2025 non-GAAP diluted EPS guidance from $20-$21.2 to $20.2-$21.3.

Therefore, I continue to cover Amgen with a Buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: S&P 500: Buy Put Spreads Before The Tide Turns
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Weidong Li, CFA as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Since the April low that followed the tariff announcement, U.S. equities have surged astonishingly in a remarkably short time. The S&P 500 (SPX) is up about 35%, the tech-heavy Nasdaq is up more than 40%, and the market continues to notch new highs almost every session. Euphoria is rampant. Because prices have far outpaced corporate earnings and economic growth, the market's underlying fundamentals have deteriorated, increasing the odds of a correction. In this backdrop-and with volatility still subdued-adding portfolio insurance through options, particularly well-chosen put spreads, looks like a sensible strategy.

Overbought market

The US equity market is hovering at an elevated level in terms of valuation; there are multiple warning signs out there, but here I would just list three commonly known measures to illustrate my point: the Shiller P/E ratio, the Buffett indicator and S&P dividend yield versus bond yield.

The Shiller P/E (or CAPE) is the market's price divided by the average of real, inflation-adjusted earnings over the past 10 years, used to smooth cycles and gauge valuation. Right now, this ratio stands at 39.4 versus a 20-year average of 27.1.

Secondly, the Buffett indicator, which compares a country's total stock market capitalization to its gross domestic product (market cap/GDP) to assess whether the equity market is over- or undervalued, stands at 218%, far above the 165% threshold for being significantly overvalued.

Third, compare the S&P 500's dividend yield with Treasuries: it sits about 2.4 percentage points below the 2-year and 2.8 points below the 10-year, signaling that equity investors are accepting far less income than risk-free bonds and are thus counting on strong earnings growth and price appreciation to supply the equity risk premium.

When putting these readings alongside historical averages, it appears quite clear to me that market valuation is overstretched. Therefore, I suspect the market's defiant upswing is powered more by investor sentiments than by economic fundamentals.

Sentiment is pushing the market higher

Since the abrupt dip in April right after the tariff shock, the market has pulled up dramatically without improving fundamentals, investing community has become saturated with positive news and rosy prospects, especially in the last couple of months, such as the anticipated Fed rate cuts, hopes for a de-escalation in trade wars, massive corporate buybacks, and an exuberant belief in an AI-driven technology revolution, etc. Together they have provided investors the courage to pay ever-higher multiples for the same stream of earnings. Prices have risen much faster than profit forecasts, suggesting multiple expansion rather than profitability improvement is keeping the party going.

One particular worrying sign that's worth pointing out is the narrowing of market leadership. A handful of mega-cap technology firms now accounts for an outsized share of index composition (see chart below), creating a self-reinforcing loop: passive ETF flows and benchmark indexing investment push more capital into market winners, which further lifts their weights in the index. The S&P 500's representation of the broader market is now heavily skewed towards the high-beta, high-P/E ratio, high-hope tech sector. This concentration could potentially amplify market volatility once the exodus begins; it's simply too crowded for everyone to unwind on time. Every step the market climbs further in this backdrop means the pendulum will strike harder once it loses upward momentum and strikes back down.

Moreover, sentiment and emotions are often sticky and contagious; they tend to be widespread and lingering for quite some time. In that process, investors' fear of missing out, reckless momentum chasing, and the false belief that "this time is different" keep market participants pouring into narrower and narrower trades. Such rallies can persist, but they are inherently fragile: when every positive expectation is already priced in, any disappointment can cause the flip quickly.

Market cyclicality

In the world of investment, few things are certain; shocks are common occurrence, and surprises are everywhere. However, the market's cyclicality is not one of them; it's quite certain that prices and valuations do not move in a straight line but oscillate through expansions and contractions. These cycles are caused by a combination of objective drivers and subjective forces. Business cycles induce fluctuations in company earnings, interest rates, liquidity, and credit quality, all of which translate into the market's waxing and waning. On the subjective side, investor sentiment, risk appetite, and behavioral biases create feedback loops that push prices away from fundamentals and then back again. The result is a recurring zig-zag path of market movement, with highly variable length and amplitude across episodes.

"History doesn't repeat itself, but it often rhymes". While no two cycles are identical, the duration and magnitude of each cycle could be very different, but the pattern of mean reversion in profit margins and valuation multiples is persistent; the reoccurrence of market ups and downs is near certain. For investors, the message is unmistakable: assuming that today's favorable conditions will persist indefinitely is a dangerous illusion. If the objective is long-term value preservation, then building portfolio insurance is not optional-it is essential.

Buying SPY put spreads as a portfolio hedge

No one could predict the exact course of market movement, yet we could all prepare for the future, and options are powerful tools for hedging portfolio positions. Particularly when volatility is subdued and premiums are relatively inexpensive, carefully constructed option strategies can provide effective downside protection in a cost-efficient manner. Based on the market analysis I have put forward, I advise the purchase of put option spreads in SPY to hedge against market correction.

I will use one example to illustrate my point. The following screenshot is the option chain of SPY in the Dec '25 expiry, taken on 16 Sep at an SPY spot price of 660.2:

A bull spread constructed with puts involves purchasing a higher strike put and simultaneously selling a lower strike put. For instance, one could buy a SPY December 2025 650 put and sell a SPY December 2025 640 put, thereby creating a long position in the SPY Dec '25 650-640 put spread. Based on current pricing, one contract of this spread costs approximately $237. The simplified payoff profile is as follows:

Maximum loss at expiry

Maximum gain at expiry

Breakeven point

$237

$763

647.63

Now, consider a market portfolio valued at $100,000. Allocating just 1% of the portfolio to this strategy would allow the purchase of four contracts of the Dec '25 650-640 put spread. The effect on portfolio outcomes under different market scenarios is shown below:

SPY

down 5%

SPY

down 3%

SPY unchanged

SPY

up 3%

SPY

up 5%

Portfolio without PS

-5%

-3%

0%

+3%

+5%

Portfolio with PS

-2%

0%

-1%

+2%

+4%

This example demonstrates how put spreads can smooth portfolio payoffs by mitigating downside losses. The strategy modestly reduces upside participation but provides meaningful protection in the event of a market correction, thereby improving the overall risk-return profile of the portfolio.

Moreover, a bull put spread is often preferable to outright put purchases when the anticipated market correction is expected to be limited. It's more cost-efficient and thus less sensitive to time decay.

A bull put spread strategy is also preferable to using VIX-tracking ETFs as a portfolio hedge, since movements in VIX ETFs do not exhibit a reliable one-to-one correlation with SPY and are further disadvantaged by negative roll yield when the VIX future term structure is in contango.

Risks of bull put spread strategy

Timing, position management, and mental resilience are crucial to this strategy's success. Getting the timing right is inherently difficult: overvaluation doesn't mean prices will fall tomorrow, and markets can stay expensive-or climb further-for quite some time. A practical approach is to anchor trades to the economic calendar: identify likely catalysts and establish put spreads around them-Federal Reserve rate decisions, monthly inflation releases, and mega-cap tech earnings, for example:

October 15 & 16, 2025

CPI & PPI for September

October 22, 2025

Earnings release TSLA (after close)

October 28, 2025

Earnings release Alphabet, MSFT (after close)

October 29, 2025

Fed Rate Decision

November 7, 2025

Non-farm Payroll data

November 13&14, 2025

CPI & PPI for October

November 19, 2025

Earnings release NVDA (after close)

December 5, 2025

Non-farm Payroll data

December 10, 2025

Fed Rate Decision

December 10&11, 2025

CPI & PPI for November

January 8, 2026

Non-farm Payroll data

January 28, 2026

Fed Rate Decision

Disciplined position management is essential. Size your put spreads so they're large enough to provide meaningful protection in a correction, yet small enough not to dent annual returns. As a rule of thumb, I would allocate about 1-2% of portfolio value to downside hedges.

Another material risk is psychological: withstanding the stress of being out of step with the herd. Maintain conviction by anchoring decisions to a written, evidence-based thesis and a predefined trading plan. Discipline is the best defense against emotional fluctuation.

Bottom Line

U.S. equities look rich and sentiment-driven, and market cycles make a drawdown likely even if timing is uncertain. A small, disciplined allocation (1-2% of portfolio value) to SPY put spreads while volatility is subdued can provide cost-effective, defined-risk protection against a typical 5-15% pullback and avoids the decay issues of VIX ETFs. Time entries around known catalysts, and follow a written plan to manage positions and emotions.

data.title: Data Center Development Opportunities In Secondary Markets
data.description:
By Casey Miller, Managing Director, Portfolio Management | Paul Lewis, Director - European Data Centres | Sebastian Dooley, CFA, Senior Fund Manager | Matt Hackman, Portfolio Manager

Demand for data centers is booming. In primary markets, available capacity is minimal, driving vacancy rates to historic lows, less than 2% in most markets. At the same time, new development has been increasingly constrained. Lead times for critical equipment such as transformers, switches and generators and long delays for permitting and power interconnection have doubled timelines in many cases.1

Now, with exponentially more demand from the AI revolution, power and land itself is in short supply in many primary markets. Suitable sites with a clear path to permitting and access to utility power are increasingly scarce (as Exhibit 1 demonstrates). In some, Dublin and Amsterdam, for example, regulators have imposed moratoriums on new data center builds due to a lack of power infrastructure.

Availability of suitable sites with a clear path to permitting and access to utility power

As we noted last year, the majority of data center capacity remains concentrated in primary markets.2 This is true in both the U.S. and Europe, though in Europe the share of capacity concentrated in primary markets has been declining since Q1 2021. We continue to invest in primary markets when appropriate sites are available for development or existing facilities become available. However, to meet the growing demand for capacity expected over the next 3-5 years, we must also explore development opportunities beyond the primary markets.

The factors we look for in a market

The fundamental factors determining data center market attractiveness have remained largely consistent throughout time. However, a market’s performance against these fundamentals may evolve over time, along with the comparative significance of individual factors. (For example, as data centers expand in scale, land and power availability become more important.)

Availability of power and land

The availability of suitable sites with a clear path to power is fundamentally critical. Permitting and access to utility power is indispensable, particularly as data center campuses expand and become more power intensive. This factor has constrained development in some markets before; New York/New Jersey and Silicon Valley are examples where power and land constraints have been an inhibiting factor for many years. Until recently, most primary markets had many suitable sites with a clear path to permitting and access to utility power. Now, power and land are constrained in many primary markets, as Exhibit 1 makes clear, and thus is the first (though not only) consideration factor for data center developers.

Cost of reliable power

Power reliability is table stakes; data center developers will not build where power is not reliable. (Although in highly power-constrained markets, we are seeing some developers willing to rely on other sources of power generation for the short term while waiting for reliable utility power interconnection.)
